book.name: Ezra
book.author: Traditionally Ezra the scribe, possibly compiled by the Chronicler
book.writtenTo: The returned Jewish community in Judah, to establish their identity as the continuation of pre-exilic Israel

explainer.overview: Ezra chronicles two pivotal moments in Jewish restoration after the Babylonian exile. The first six chapters describe the initial return under Zerubbabel (538 BC), the rebuilding of the temple, and opposition from local enemies. Chapters 7-10 jump forward about 60 years to Ezra's own mission (458 BC): a priestly scribe sent by the Persian king Artaxerxes to teach God's law and reform the community. The book culminates with Ezra's painful confrontation of intermarriage between Jews and surrounding peoples, leading to a communal covenant of repentance.
explainer.historicalContext: The Babylonian exile ended in 538 BC when Cyrus the Great of Persia conquered Babylon and issued a decree allowing exiled peoples to return home and rebuild their temples. The returning Jews faced enormous challenges: a destroyed temple, hostile neighbors, economic hardship, and the gradual erosion of religious identity through intermarriage and cultural assimilation. Ezra's reforms, supported by Persian imperial authority, sought to re-establish Torah observance as the foundation of Jewish community life.
explainer.whyItMatters: Ezra marks the transition from ancient Israel to Second Temple Judaism -- the form of Jewish faith that produced the synagogue, the scribal tradition, and the religious world into which Jesus was born. Ezra's emphasis on Torah study, communal purity, and covenant renewal shaped Judaism permanently. The book raises enduring questions about how faith communities maintain their identity in a pluralistic world, the role of religious authority, and the tension between inclusion and distinctiveness.
keyThemes: God's faithfulness to restore his people
The centrality of the temple and Torah
The danger of assimilation and intermarriage
Persian imperial support as divine providence

people.name: Ezra
people.role: Priest, scribe, teacher of the Law, leader of the second return
people.bio: Ezra was a priest descended from Aaron through the high-priestly line and a scribe skilled in the Law of Moses. He led a second wave of exiles from Babylon to Jerusalem in 458 BC with a royal commission from Artaxerxes to teach God's law and appoint judges. Upon arriving, he discovered widespread intermarriage with surrounding peoples and led a dramatic communal repentance. He is traditionally credited with finalizing the Old Testament canon and establishing the synagogue system.
people.culturalContext: Ezra represents the emergence of the 'scribe' as a religious authority figure alongside priests. His combination of priestly lineage and scribal expertise made him uniquely qualified to lead religious reform. Jewish tradition considers him second only to Moses in his contribution to Torah observance.

people.name: Zerubbabel
people.role: Governor of Judah, leader of the first return, temple builder
people.bio: Zerubbabel was a descendant of King David through Jehoiachin, making him the legitimate heir to the Davidic throne. He led the first wave of returning exiles from Babylon to Jerusalem in 538 BC and oversaw the rebuilding of the temple despite fierce opposition. The prophets Haggai and Zechariah both recognized him as a messianic figure.
people.culturalContext: As a Davidic descendant serving as Persian-appointed governor, Zerubbabel embodied the tension between Jewish royal hopes and political reality. He ruled not as a king but as a vassal, yet the prophets saw in him the continuation of God's covenant with David.

people.name: Cyrus the Great
people.role: King of Persia, instrument of Jewish restoration
people.bio: Cyrus II of Persia conquered Babylon in 539 BC and issued a decree allowing exiled peoples to return to their homelands and rebuild their temples. His policy of religious tolerance was revolutionary. Isaiah 45:1 calls him God's 'anointed' (mashiach) -- the only non-Jew given this title in the Bible.
people.culturalContext: Cyrus's decree is confirmed by the Cyrus Cylinder, an archaeological artifact discovered in 1879. His policy of tolerant rule through local religious restoration was a strategic innovation that secured loyalty across a diverse empire.

people.name: Artaxerxes I
people.role: King of Persia who authorized Ezra's mission
people.bio: Artaxerxes I (Longimanus) reigned 465-424 BC. He authorized both Ezra's religious mission (458 BC) and Nehemiah's rebuilding of Jerusalem's walls (445 BC). His support for Jewish restoration continued the Persian policy established by Cyrus.
people.culturalContext: Artaxerxes ruled during a period of occasional revolt in the western provinces. Supporting Jewish loyalty through religious authorization was politically shrewd, ensuring a friendly buffer community near Egypt.

places.name: Jerusalem
places.description: The ancient capital of Judah, site of Solomon's temple, destroyed by Babylon in 586 BC and gradually rebuilt by the returning exiles.
places.culturalSignificance: Jerusalem is the destination of both returns and the site of temple rebuilding. Its restoration from ruins symbolizes God's faithfulness to his promises and the continuity of the covenant.

places.name: Babylon
places.description: Capital of the Babylonian Empire, where Jews lived in exile from 586 BC. After Persia conquered Babylon (539 BC), it became a provincial capital with a large Jewish community.
places.culturalSignificance: Babylon is the starting point for both waves of returning exiles. The Jewish community in Babylon remained large and influential even after the returns, eventually producing the Babylonian Talmud centuries later.

places.name: The Second Temple
places.description: The rebuilt temple in Jerusalem, completed in 516 BC. It replaced Solomon's temple destroyed by Babylon in 586 BC.
places.culturalSignificance: The rebuilding of the temple is the central project of Ezra 1-6. The Second Temple would stand until its destruction by Rome in 70 AD, serving as the center of Jewish worship for nearly 600 years.

First return under Zerubbabel and Jeshua; altar rebuilt; temple foundation laid
Temple construction halted due to opposition from local peoples and political interference
Temple construction resumes under Haggai and Zechariah's encouragement; completed and dedicated in 516 BC
Ezra leads a second return from Babylon with Artaxerxes' letter of authorization
Ezra confronts intermarriage; communal repentance and covenant renewal

explainer.summary: In the first year of Cyrus king of Persia (538 BC), God stirs the king's heart to issue a decree allowing Jews to return to Jerusalem and rebuild the temple. Cyrus not only permits the return but provides funding and returns the temple vessels Nebuchadnezzar had looted. The Jewish community in Babylon rallies with offerings, and the treasured temple vessels are counted and entrusted to Sheshbazzar, prince of Judah.
themes: God stirring a pagan king's heart
explainer.contextInBook: This opening chapter sets the theological tone: God is sovereignly orchestrating the restoration of his people through a pagan emperor. Cyrus's decree fulfills Jeremiah's prophecy of a 70-year exile and demonstrates that no empire is beyond God's control.

wordStudies.significance: God 'stirred up' the spirit of Cyrus (v.1). The same verb describes God rousing human agents throughout the Old Testament, showing divine initiative working through human decisions.
Cyrus's decree goes out as a 'voice' (qol) throughout his kingdom (v.1). The same word for God's prophetic voice now comes through a pagan king.
The community strengthens the returnees with freewill offerings (v.4, 6). The voluntary nature emphasizes that restoration flows from willing hearts, not compulsion.

verses.text:
Now in the first year of Cyrus king of Persia, that Yahweh’s word
 by Jeremiah’s mouth might be accomplished, Yahweh stirred up the spirit of Cyrus king of Persia, so that he made a proclamation throughout all his kingdom, and put it also in writing, saying,
verses.explainer: The book opens with the fulfillment of prophecy: in Cyrus's first year over Babylon (538 BC), Yahweh stirs the king's spirit to issue a decree for rebuilding the Jerusalem temple, just as Jeremiah had predicted.

annotations.word: the first year of Cyrus king of Persia
Yahweh stirred up the spirit of Cyrus

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The Cyrus Cylinder, discovered in 1879, confirms Cyrus's policy of restoring exiled peoples and their temples. What the Bible presents as divine initiative, Cyrus presented as the will of the Babylonian god Marduk.

crossReferences.connection: God's promise: 'After seventy years are accomplished for Babylon, I will visit you and perform my good word toward you, causing you to return.'
Isaiah names Cyrus by name over a century before his birth, calling him God's 'shepherd' and 'anointed' (mashiach).

thenVsNow.then: Then, God used a Persian emperor who did not know him to fulfill prophecy and restore his people. Today, God still works through secular authorities, political changes, and unexpected events to accomplish his purposes.
thenVsNow.now: Then, God used a Persian emperor who did not know him to fulfill prophecy and restore his people. Today, God still works through secular authorities, political changes, and unexpected events to accomplish his purposes.

verses.text: “Thus says Cyrus king of Persia, ‘Yahweh, the God of heaven, has given me all the kingdoms of the earth; and he has commanded me to build him a house in Jerusalem, which is in Judah.
verses.explainer: Cyrus's decree acknowledges Yahweh as the God of heaven who gave him his kingdom and charged him to build a temple in Jerusalem. The pagan king gives credit to Israel's God.

verses.text: Whoever there is among you of all his people, may his God be with him, and let him go up to Jerusalem, which is in Judah, and build the house of Yahweh, the God of Israel (he is God), which is in Jerusalem.
verses.explainer: The decree invites any Jew throughout the empire to return to Jerusalem and rebuild the temple. God's presence ('may his God be with him') is invoked as blessing.

verses.text: Whoever is left, in any place where he lives, let the men of his place help him with silver, with gold, with goods, and with animals, besides the freewill offering for God’s house which is in Jerusalem.’”
verses.explainer: Those who choose not to return are commanded to support the returnees with silver, gold, goods, animals, and freewill offerings. The community collectively funds the restoration.

annotations.word: let the men of his place help him

crossReferences.connection: Just as Israel left Egypt with Egyptian wealth, the returning exiles leave Babylon with contributions from surrounding peoples.

verses.text: Then the heads of fathers’ households of Judah and Benjamin, the priests, and the Levites, all whose spirit God had stirred to go up rose up to build Yahweh’s house which is in Jerusalem.
verses.explainer: The heads of families from Judah and Benjamin, along with priests and Levites, prepare to go -- specifically those whose spirits God had stirred. Not everyone goes; the return is selective.

verses.text: All those who were around them strengthened their hands with vessels of silver, with gold, with goods, with animals, and with precious things, besides all that was willingly offered.
verses.explainer: Neighbors support the returnees with silver, gold, goods, livestock, and precious gifts -- a voluntary outpouring that funds the restoration effort.

verses.text: Also Cyrus the king brought out the vessels of Yahweh’s house, which Nebuchadnezzar had brought out of Jerusalem, and had put in the house of his gods;
verses.explainer: Cyrus personally orders the return of the temple vessels that Nebuchadnezzar had looted from Jerusalem and placed in the temple of his gods in Babylon.

annotations.word: Nebuchadnezzar had brought out of Jerusalem

crossReferences.connection: Belshazzar's sacrilegious use of the temple vessels at his feast precipitated Babylon's fall -- the very event that enabled their return.

verses.text: even those, Cyrus king of Persia brought out by the hand of Mithredath the treasurer, and counted them out to Sheshbazzar, the prince of Judah.
verses.explainer: Mithredath the treasurer oversees the transfer of the vessels to Sheshbazzar, identified as prince of Judah. Every item is carefully counted and documented.

verses.text: This is the number of them: thirty platters of gold, one thousand platters of silver, twenty-nine knives,
verses.explainer: The detailed inventory begins: 30 gold platters, 1,000 silver platters, 29 knives. The precise counting emphasizes the sacred importance of these objects.

verses.text: thirty bowls of gold, four hundred ten silver bowls of a second sort, and one thousand other vessels.
verses.explainer: The inventory continues: 30 gold bowls, 410 silver bowls of a second kind, and 1,000 other vessels.

verses.text: All the vessels of gold and of silver were five thousand four hundred. Sheshbazzar brought all these up when the captives were brought up from Babylon to Jerusalem.
verses.explainer: The total: 5,400 vessels of gold and silver. Sheshbazzar brings them all up when the exiles return from Babylon to Jerusalem.

crossReferences.connection: Isaiah prophesied: 'Depart, depart, go out from there... you who carry the vessels of Yahweh.' The return of the vessels fulfills this prophecy.

explainer.summary: A detailed census of the returnees, organized by family, town of origin, priestly and Levitical divisions, and temple servants. The total comes to 42,360 free persons plus 7,337 servants and 200 singers. Some families cannot prove their genealogy and are excluded from priestly service. The people contribute generously to the rebuilding fund.
explainer.contextInBook: This chapter, largely a census list, serves a vital purpose: it establishes that the returning community is the legitimate continuation of pre-exilic Israel. Every name connects the present to the past, proving that God has preserved his people through exile.

wordStudies.significance: The returnees are called 'children of the province' (v.1) -- the Persian administrative term for Judah. What was once an independent kingdom is now merely a province.

wordStudies.translatedAs: To be enrolled by genealogy, to prove one's lineag
wordStudies.meaning: To be enrolled by genealogy, to prove one's lineage
wordStudies.significance: Some who 'sought their register among those who were reckoned by genealogy' could not find it (v.62). Genealogical documentation was essential for priestly service and community membership.

verses.text: Now these are the children of the province, who went up out of the captivity of those who had been carried away, whom Nebuchadnezzar the king of Babylon had carried away to Babylon, and who returned to Jerusalem and Judah, everyone to his city;
verses.explainer: The returnees are identified as those who had been carried away by Nebuchadnezzar and now return to Jerusalem and Judah, each to their own town. The connection to the exile is explicit.

verses.text:
who came with Zerubbabel, Jeshua, Nehemiah, Seraiah, Reelaiah, Mordecai, Bilshan, Mispar, Bigvai, Rehum, and Baanah.
The number of the men of the people of Israel:
verses.explainer: The leaders of the return are listed. Zerubbabel heads the list as the Davidic leader, followed by Jeshua (the high priest) and nine other leaders representing the community's civic and religious structure.

crossReferences.connection: Haggai addresses both Zerubbabel (governor) and Joshua/Jeshua (high priest) as the dual leaders of the restoration community.

verses.text: The children of Parosh, two thousand one hundred seventy-two.
verses.explainer: The census begins listing families by their ancestral patriarchs: the children of Parosh (2,172 people). These family groupings preserve pre-exilic social structures.

verses.text: The children of Shephatiah, three hundred seventy-two.
verses.explainer: The descendants of Shephatiah: 372 people. Each family group is recorded to establish continuity with pre-exilic Israel.

verses.text: The children of Arah, seven hundred seventy-five.
verses.explainer: The descendants of Arah: 775 people. The varying sizes of family groups reflect the uneven survival and return rates of different clans.

verses.text: The children of Pahathmoab, of the children of Jeshua and Joab, two thousand eight hundred twelve.
verses.explainer: The descendants of Pahath-moab, through the lines of Jeshua and Joab: 2,812. This was one of the largest family groups returning.

verses.text: The children of Elam, one thousand two hundred fifty-four.
verses.explainer: The descendants of Elam: 1,254. The name Elam connects to the region east of Babylon, suggesting some intermarriage with local populations during exile.

verses.text: The children of Zattu, nine hundred forty-five.
verses.explainer: The descendants of Zattu: 945.

verses.text: The children of Zaccai, seven hundred sixty.
verses.explainer: The descendants of Zaccai: 760.

verses.text: The children of Bani, six hundred forty-two.
verses.explainer: The descendants of Bani: 642.

verses.text: The children of Bebai, six hundred twenty-three.
verses.explainer: The descendants of Bebai: 623.

verses.text: The children of Azgad, one thousand two hundred twenty-two.
verses.explainer: The descendants of Azgad: 1,222. Another large family group committed to the return.

verses.text: The children of Adonikam, six hundred sixty-six.
verses.explainer: The descendants of Adonikam: 666.

verses.text: The children of Bigvai, two thousand fifty-six.
verses.explainer: The descendants of Bigvai: 2,056. One of the largest returning family groups.

verses.text: The children of Adin, four hundred fifty-four.
verses.explainer: The descendants of Adin: 454.

verses.text: The children of Ater, of Hezekiah, ninety-eight.
verses.explainer: The descendants of Ater through Hezekiah: 98. A smaller family identified through a notable ancestor.

verses.text: The children of Bezai, three hundred twenty-three.
verses.explainer: The descendants of Bezai: 323.

verses.text: The children of Jorah, one hundred twelve.
verses.explainer: The descendants of Jorah: 112. One of the smaller returning family groups.

verses.text: The children of Hashum, two hundred twenty-three.
verses.explainer: The descendants of Hashum: 223.

verses.text: The children of Gibbar, ninety-five.
verses.explainer: The descendants of Gibbar: 95. Alternatively identified as Gibeon in the Nehemiah parallel.

verses.text: The children of Bethlehem, one hundred twenty-three.
verses.explainer: The people of Bethlehem: 123. From verse 21 onward, the census shifts from family names to towns of origin.

verses.text: The men of Netophah, fifty-six.
verses.explainer: The men of Netophah: 56. A small village near Bethlehem.

verses.text: The men of Anathoth, one hundred twenty-eight.
verses.explainer: The men of Anathoth: 128. Anathoth was the hometown of the prophet Jeremiah.

verses.text: The children of Azmaveth, forty-two.
verses.explainer: The descendants of Azmaveth: 42.

verses.text: The children of Kiriath Arim, Chephirah, and Beeroth, seven hundred forty-three.
verses.explainer: The people of Kiriath-arim, Chephirah, and Beeroth combined: 743. Three Gibeonite towns counted together.

verses.text: The children of Ramah and Geba, six hundred twenty-one.
verses.explainer: The people of Ramah and Geba: 621. Two Benjaminite towns counted together.

verses.text: The men of Michmas, one hundred twenty-two.
verses.explainer: The men of Michmas: 122. A strategically located town in Benjamin.

verses.text: The men of Bethel and Ai, two hundred twenty-three.
verses.explainer: The men of Bethel and Ai: 223. Two towns famous from Joshua's conquest, now repopulated.

verses.text: The children of Nebo, fifty-two.
verses.explainer: The descendants of Nebo: 52.

verses.text: The children of Magbish, one hundred fifty-six.
verses.explainer: The descendants of Magbish: 156. This name does not appear in the Nehemiah parallel list.

verses.text: The children of the other Elam, one thousand two hundred fifty-four.
verses.explainer: The descendants of the other Elam: 1,254. The same number as verse 7, possibly a different branch of the same extended family.

verses.text: The children of Harim, three hundred twenty.
verses.explainer: The descendants of Harim: 320.

verses.text: The children of Lod, Hadid, and Ono, seven hundred twenty-five.
verses.explainer: The descendants of Lod, Hadid, and Ono: 725. Three towns in the western lowland of Benjamin.

verses.text: The children of Jericho, three hundred forty-five.
verses.explainer: The descendants of Jericho: 345. The ancient city now yields a modest number of returnees.

verses.text: The children of Senaah, three thousand six hundred thirty.
verses.explainer: The descendants of Senaah: 3,630. By far the largest single group, possibly representing the common people without distinguished family names.

verses.text: The priests: the children of Jedaiah, of the house of Jeshua, nine hundred seventy-three.
verses.explainer: The priests are now listed. The descendants of Jedaiah from the house of Jeshua: 973. This priestly family was associated with the high-priestly line.

annotations.word: the children of Jedaiah, of the house of Jeshua

verses.text: The children of Immer, one thousand fifty-two.
verses.explainer: The descendants of Immer: 1,052. A major priestly division.

verses.text: The children of Pashhur, one thousand two hundred forty-seven.
verses.explainer: The descendants of Pashhur: 1,247. Another large priestly family.

verses.text: The children of Harim, one thousand seventeen.
verses.explainer: The descendants of Harim: 1,017. The four priestly families totaled 4,289 -- roughly 10% of the returnees.

verses.text: The Levites: the children of Jeshua and Kadmiel, of the children of Hodaviah, seventy-four.
verses.explainer: The Levites: the descendants of Jeshua and Kadmiel, from Hodaviah's line: only 74. The tiny number of returning Levites is a recurring problem in Ezra-Nehemiah.

verses.text: The singers: the children of Asaph, one hundred twenty-eight.
verses.explainer: The singers descended from Asaph: 128. These temple musicians were essential for worship liturgy.

verses.text: The children of the gatekeepers: the children of Shallum, the children of Ater, the children of Talmon, the children of Akkub, the children of Hatita, the children of Shobai, in all one hundred thirty-nine.
verses.explainer: The gatekeepers descended from six families: 139 total. Gatekeepers controlled access to the temple and maintained security.

verses.text: The temple servants: the children of Ziha, the children of Hasupha, the children of Tabbaoth,
verses.explainer: The temple servants (Nethinim) are listed by family. These were non-Israelite servants dedicated to temple service, some descended from the Gibeonites.

verses.text: the children of Keros, the children of Siaha, the children of Padon,
verses.explainer: Additional families of temple servants are listed: Keros, Siaha, and Padon.

verses.text: the children of Lebanah, the children of Hagabah, the children of Akkub,
verses.explainer: More temple servant families: Lebanah, Hagabah, and Akkub.

verses.text: the children of Hagab, the children of Shamlai, the children of Hanan,
verses.explainer: Additional temple servant families: Hagab, Shamlai, and Hanan.

verses.text: the children of Giddel, the children of Gahar, the children of Reaiah,
verses.explainer: More families: Giddel, Gahar, and Reaiah.

verses.text: the children of Rezin, the children of Nekoda, the children of Gazzam,
verses.explainer: Additional families: Rezin, Nekoda, and Gazzam.

verses.text: the children of Uzza, the children of Paseah, the children of Besai,
verses.explainer: More families: Uzza, Paseah, and Besai.

verses.text: the children of Asnah, the children of Meunim, the children of Nephisim,
verses.explainer: Additional families: Asnah, Meunim, and Nephisim.

verses.text: the children of Bakbuk, the children of Hakupha, the children of Harhur,
verses.explainer: More families: Bakbuk, Hakupha, and Harhur.

verses.text: the children of Bazluth, the children of Mehida, the children of Harsha,
verses.explainer: Additional families: Bazluth, Mehida, and Harsha.

verses.text: the children of Barkos, the children of Sisera, the children of Temah,
verses.explainer: More families: Barkos, Sisera, and Temah.

verses.text: the children of Neziah, the children of Hatipha.
verses.explainer: The final temple servant families: Neziah and Hatipha. The list is thorough and complete.

verses.text: The children of Solomon’s servants: the children of Sotai, the children of Hassophereth, the children of Peruda,
verses.explainer: The descendants of Solomon's servants are now listed, beginning with Sotai, Hassophereth, and Peruda. These were descendants of Canaanites whom Solomon conscripted for state labor.

crossReferences.connection: Solomon enslaved the remaining Canaanite populations for forced labor. Their descendants continued serving in the temple community.

verses.text: the children of Jaalah, the children of Darkon, the children of Giddel,
verses.explainer: More of Solomon's servants' descendants: Jaalah, Darkon, and Giddel.

verses.text: the children of Shephatiah, the children of Hattil, the children of Pochereth Hazzebaim, the children of Ami.
verses.explainer: Final descendants of Solomon's servants: Shephatiah, Hattil, Pochereth-hazzebaim, and Ami.

verses.text: All the temple servants, and the children of Solomon’s servants, were three hundred ninety-two.
verses.explainer: Total of temple servants and Solomon's servants' descendants: 392. Though small in number, these service workers were essential to temple operations.

verses.text: These were those who went up from Tel Melah, Tel Harsha, Cherub, Addan, and Immer; but they could not show their fathers’ houses, and their offspring, whether they were of Israel:
verses.explainer: Some returnees from Tel-melah, Tel-harsha, and other Babylonian locations could not prove their Israelite ancestry. Their genealogical records were incomplete or lost.

verses.text: the children of Delaiah, the children of Tobiah, the children of Nekoda, six hundred fifty-two.
verses.explainer: Specifically, the families of Delaiah, Tobiah, and Nekoda -- 652 people -- could not prove their Israelite descent.

verses.text: Of the children of the priests: the children of Habaiah, the children of Hakkoz, and the children of Barzillai, who took a wife of the daughters of Barzillai the Gileadite, and was called after their name.
verses.explainer: Even more seriously, some priestly families -- Habaiah, Hakkoz, and Barzillai (who married a daughter of Barzillai the Gileadite) -- could not prove their priestly lineage.

verses.text: These sought their place among those who were registered by genealogy, but they were not found: therefore were they deemed disqualified and removed from the priesthood.
verses.explainer: These families searched for their genealogical records but could not find them. They were excluded from the priesthood as unclean -- unable to serve at the altar.

verses.text: The governor told them that they should not eat of the most holy things until a priest stood up to serve with Urim and with Thummim.
verses.explainer: The governor ruled that they could not eat the priests' portions until a priest could consult the Urim and Thummim -- the sacred lots used for divine guidance. This deferred the decision to God.

crossReferences.connection: The Urim and Thummim were placed in the high priest's breastplate for making decisions requiring divine guidance.

verses.text: The whole assembly together was forty-two thousand three hundred sixty,
verses.explainer: The total assembly: 42,360 people, besides their servants. This number represented a small fraction of the Jewish population in the Persian Empire -- most chose not to return.

thenVsNow.then: Then, only a remnant returned from exile -- most Jews stayed in the comfort of Babylon. Today, answering God's call often requires leaving comfortable situations, and many choose the easier path.
thenVsNow.now: Then, only a remnant returned from exile -- most Jews stayed in the comfort of Babylon. Today, answering God's call often requires leaving comfortable situations, and many choose the easier path.

verses.text: besides their male servants and their female servants, of whom there were seven thousand three hundred thirty-seven; and they had two hundred singing men and singing women.
verses.explainer: An additional 7,337 male and female servants, plus 200 male and female singers. The servants were likely non-Israelite household workers.

annotations.word: two hundred singing men and singing women

verses.text: Their horses were seven hundred thirty-six; their mules, two hundred forty-five;
verses.explainer: Their livestock: 736 horses, 245 mules. The number of animals indicates the relative wealth of the returning community.

verses.text: their camels, four hundred thirty-five; their donkeys, six thousand seven hundred twenty.
verses.explainer: Also 435 camels and 6,720 donkeys. The large number of donkeys (the common person's transport) shows that ordinary families made the journey alongside the wealthy.

verses.text: Some of the heads of fathers’ households, when they came to Yahweh’s house which is in Jerusalem, offered willingly for God’s house to set it up in its place.
verses.explainer: When they arrive at the temple site, some family heads make freewill offerings for the rebuilding. Their generosity is spontaneous and voluntary.

crossReferences.connection: David's leaders gave voluntarily for the first temple. The returnees follow the same pattern for the second.

verses.text: They gave according to their ability into the treasury of the work sixty-one thousand darics of gold, and five thousand minas of silver, and one hundred priests’ garments.
verses.explainer: The contributions totaled 61,000 gold drachmas, 5,000 silver minas, and 100 priestly garments. These funds would launch the temple reconstruction.

verses.text: So the priests, and the Levites, and some of the people, the singers, the gatekeepers, and the temple servants, lived in their cities, and all Israel in their cities.
verses.explainer: The community settles: priests, Levites, singers, gatekeepers, temple servants, and ordinary Israelites all take up residence in their assigned towns throughout Judah.

explainer.summary: In the seventh month, the people gather in Jerusalem. Jeshua and Zerubbabel rebuild the altar and resume daily sacrifices and festival observances. They celebrate the Feast of Tabernacles. In the second year, they lay the temple foundation with great ceremony -- the priests trumpet, Levites crash cymbals, and the people shout with joy. But the old men who remembered Solomon's temple weep at how inferior this one is. The mingled sounds of joy and weeping carry far.
themes: Restoring worship before rebuilding the temple
explainer.contextInBook: This chapter shows that worship comes before building. The altar is rebuilt and sacrifices resume even before the temple foundation is laid. The mixed emotions at the foundation ceremony -- young people shouting with joy, old people weeping with memory -- capture the bittersweet nature of restoration.

wordStudies.translatedAs: To found, establish, lay a foundation
wordStudies.meaning: To found, establish, lay a foundation
wordStudies.significance: The temple foundation (v.10-11) uses yasad, a word that also means to establish or ordain. Laying the foundation is both a construction act and a theological statement of re-establishment.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Shout of joy, trumpet blast, war cry
wordStudies.meaning: Shout of joy, trumpet blast, war cry
wordStudies.significance: The 'shout' at the foundation (v.11-13) uses teruah, the same word for the shout at Jericho's walls (Joshua 6:5). The building shout echoes the conquest shout.

verses.text: When the seventh month had come, and the children of Israel were in the cities, the people gathered themselves together as one man to Jerusalem.
verses.explainer: In the seventh month (Tishri), the people gather as one in Jerusalem. The seventh month contained the Feast of Trumpets, Day of Atonement, and Feast of Tabernacles -- the holiest month.

verses.text: Then Jeshua the son of Jozadak stood up with his brothers the priests, and Zerubbabel the son of Shealtiel and his brothers, and built the altar of the God of Israel, to offer burnt offerings on it, as it is written in the law of Moses the man of God.
verses.explainer: Jeshua the high priest and Zerubbabel lead the rebuilding of the altar on its original site. They offer burnt offerings as prescribed in the Law of Moses. Worship resumes before the temple exists.

annotations.word: the altar of the God of Israel
as it is written in the law of Moses

crossReferences.connection: The law required sacrifices at the place God chose -- Jerusalem. Rebuilding the altar there restores legitimate worship.

thenVsNow.then: Then, the first priority of the returning exiles was restoring worship, even before building the temple. Today, this challenges faith communities to prioritize worship and spiritual life over buildings and programs.
thenVsNow.now: Then, the first priority of the returning exiles was restoring worship, even before building the temple. Today, this challenges faith communities to prioritize worship and spiritual life over buildings and programs.

verses.text: In spite of their fear because of the peoples of the surrounding lands, they set the altar on its base; and they offered burnt offerings on it to Yahweh, even burnt offerings morning and evening.
verses.explainer: Despite fear of the surrounding peoples, the community sets the altar on its original foundation and begins morning and evening sacrifices. Their fear drives them toward God, not away from him.

crossReferences.connection: The daily burnt offering, commanded at Sinai, is the foundational act of regular worship now resumed after decades of silence.

verses.text: They kept the feast of tents, as it is written, and offered the daily burnt offerings by number, according to the ordinance, as the duty of every day required;
verses.explainer: They celebrate the Feast of Tabernacles (Sukkot) with the prescribed daily burnt offerings. The festival commemorated wilderness wandering -- fitting for a community that has just completed its own journey through exile.

verses.text: and afterward the continual burnt offering, the offerings of the new moons, of all the set feasts of Yahweh that were consecrated, and of everyone who willingly offered a freewill offering to Yahweh.
verses.explainer: Beyond the festivals, the community resumes the regular burnt offerings, new moon sacrifices, and all the appointed feasts. The entire sacrificial calendar is restored.

verses.text: From the first day of the seventh month, they began to offer burnt offerings to Yahweh; but the foundation of Yahweh’s temple was not yet laid.
verses.explainer: Sacrifices begin on the first day of the seventh month, even though the temple foundation has not yet been laid. Worship cannot wait for construction to finish.

verses.text: They also gave money to the masons, and to the carpenters. They also gave food, drink, and oil to the people of Sidon and Tyre, to bring cedar trees from Lebanon to the sea, to Joppa, according to the grant that they had from Cyrus King of Persia.
verses.explainer: The community hires masons and carpenters and arranges for cedar wood from Lebanon, just as Solomon did for the first temple. Cyrus's decree authorizes and funds the effort.

crossReferences.connection: Solomon contracted with Hiram of Tyre for Lebanese cedar. The returnees follow the same pattern, emphasizing continuity with the first temple.

verses.text: Now in the second year of their coming to God’s house at Jerusalem, in the second month, Zerubbabel the son of Shealtiel, and Jeshua the son of Jozadak, and the rest of their brothers the priests and the Levites, and all those who had come out of the captivity to Jerusalem, began the work and appointed the Levites, from twenty years old and upward, to have the oversight of the work of Yahweh’s house.
verses.explainer: In the second year after arrival (536 BC), they begin the temple construction under the leadership of Zerubbabel, Jeshua, and the Levites aged twenty and older.

crossReferences.connection: Solomon began building the first temple in the second month. The second temple begins in the same month, creating a deliberate parallel.

verses.text: Then Jeshua stood with his sons and his brothers, Kadmiel and his sons, the sons of Judah, together, to have the oversight of the workmen in God’s house: the sons of Henadad, with their sons and their brothers the Levites.
verses.explainer: Jeshua and his sons and brothers, together with Kadmiel and the Levites, take charge of supervising the temple workers. The priestly and Levitical organization is functioning.

verses.text: When the builders laid the foundation of Yahweh’s temple, they set the priests in their clothing with trumpets, with the Levites the sons of Asaph with cymbals, to praise Yahweh, according to the directions of David king of Israel.
verses.explainer: When the foundation is laid, the priests in their vestments blow trumpets, and Levites with cymbals lead the people in praising God. They sing the ancient refrain of God's enduring love.

verses.text: They sang to one another in praising and giving thanks to Yahweh, “For he is good, for his loving kindness endures forever toward Israel.” All the people shouted with a great shout, when they praised Yahweh, because the foundation of Yahweh’s house had been laid.
verses.explainer: The people sing responsively: 'He is good, for his loving kindness endures forever toward Israel.' The great shout of praise echoes across the surrounding area.

crossReferences.connection: The perpetual refrain 'his loving kindness endures forever' was the centerpiece of Israelite liturgical worship, now restored after decades of silence.

verses.text: But many of the priests and Levites and heads of fathers’ households, the old men who had seen the first house, when the foundation of this house was laid before their eyes, wept with a loud voice. Many also shouted aloud for joy,
verses.explainer: But many older priests, Levites, and family heads who had seen Solomon's original temple weep aloud when they see this foundation. They know the second temple will be far less grand.

crossReferences.connection: Haggai asks: 'Who is left among you who saw this house in its former glory? How do you see it now? Isn't it in your eyes as nothing?'

thenVsNow.then: Then, the older generation mourned because the second temple could not match Solomon's glory. Today, communities rebuilding after loss often face the same tension between gratitude for progress and grief for what was lost.

verses.text: so that the people could not discern the noise of the shout of joy from the noise of the weeping of the people; for the people shouted with a loud shout, and the noise was heard far away.
verses.explainer: The noise is so great -- joy and weeping blended together -- that observers cannot distinguish between the shouts of joy and the cries of grief. The sound carries for miles.

explainer.summary: Enemies of the returnees offer to help build the temple, but Zerubbabel refuses, recognizing their intentions are political, not spiritual. The enemies then actively oppose the work through intimidation, political manipulation, and letters to the Persian king accusing Jerusalem of being a rebellious city. The work is forcibly stopped and remains halted until the second year of Darius (520 BC).
explainer.contextInBook: This chapter introduces the pattern of opposition that runs through Ezra-Nehemiah. Enemies use every available tool -- deceptive friendship, intimidation, political lobbying, and legal obstruction -- to stop the restoration. The result is a 16-year delay in temple construction (536-520 BC).

wordStudies.translatedAs: Adversary, enemy, one who causes trouble

wordStudies.significance: The 'adversaries of Judah and Benjamin' (v.1) defines the opponents by their hostility, not their ethnicity. Their opposition is fundamentally spiritual, not merely political.
The enemies 'weakened the hands' of the builders (v.4), a phrase meaning to discourage or demoralize. The attack targets morale as much as material progress.

verses.text: Now when the adversaries of Judah and Benjamin heard that the children of the captivity were building a temple to Yahweh, the God of Israel;

verses.text: Then the people of the land weakened the hands of the people of Judah, and troubled them in building.

verses.text: In the reign of Ahasuerus, in the beginning of his reign, they wrote an accusation against the inhabitants of Judah and Jerusalem.

verses.text: In the days of Artaxerxes, Bishlam, Mithredath, Tabeel, and the rest of his companions, wrote to Artaxerxes king of Persia; and the writing of the letter was written in Syrian, and delivered in the Syrian language.

explainer.summary: The prophets Haggai and Zechariah inspire the people to resume building. When the Persian governor Tattenai challenges their authority, the Jews cite Cyrus's original decree. Tattenai writes to King Darius asking him to verify whether such a decree exists in the archives.
explainer.contextInBook: After 16 years of inactivity, prophetic fire reignites the building project. The challenge from the provincial governor becomes an opportunity: the investigation will uncover Cyrus's decree and lead to even stronger imperial support.

wordStudies.meaning: To prophesy, speak under divine inspiration

explainer.summary: King Darius searches the archives and discovers Cyrus's original decree at Ecbatana. He issues a new decree: not only must the construction continue, but the Persian government must fund it from regional taxes. Anyone who interferes will be impaled. The temple is completed in 516 BC, exactly 70 years after its destruction, and dedicated with great celebration. The people then celebrate Passover.
themes: God vindicating his people through legal process
explainer.contextInBook: The opposition that tried to stop the temple through political means is now reversed by the same political system. God uses the Persian archives, bureaucracy, and king to not only allow but fund the temple's completion. The dedication and Passover celebration mark the full restoration of worship.

wordStudies.significance: Darius issues a te'em (decree) that goes far beyond merely permitting construction -- he commands government funding and threatens death to anyone who interferes (v.11-12).
God turned the Assyrian king's heart 'to strengthen their hands in the work' with 'joy' (v.22). The joy of completed restoration contrasts with the weeping of 3:12.

verses.text: Then Darius the king made a decree, and the house of the archives, where the treasures were laid up in Babylon, was searched.
verses.explainer: Darius orders a search of the archives in Babylon, where royal records and treasures are stored. The bureaucratic machinery that stopped the work will now authorize it.

verses.text: A scroll was found at Achmetha, in the palace that is in the province of Media, and in it this was written for a record:
verses.explainer: The decree is found not in Babylon but in Ecbatana, the summer capital of the Persian kings in the Median highlands. The scroll contains Cyrus's original authorization.

verses.text: In the first year of Cyrus the king, Cyrus the king made a decree: Concerning God’s house at Jerusalem, let the house be built, the place where they offer sacrifices, and let its foundations be strongly laid; with its height sixty cubits, and its width sixty cubits;
verses.explainer: The scroll records Cyrus's decree from his first year: the Jerusalem temple shall be rebuilt as a place of sacrifice, with dimensions of 60 cubits high and 60 cubits wide.

verses.text: with three courses of great stones and a course of new timber. Let the expenses be given out of the king’s house.

verses.explainer: The decree also orders the return of all gold and silver temple vessels to their proper places in the Jerusalem temple.

explainer.summary: The narrative jumps forward nearly 60 years to 458 BC. Ezra the priest and scribe is introduced with his impressive priestly genealogy tracing back to Aaron. King Artaxerxes grants him a remarkable letter of authorization to lead a second return to Jerusalem, teach God's law, appoint judges, and enforce Torah observance with full imperial backing -- including the death penalty for disobedience.

explainer.contextInBook: Chapters 7-10 form the second half of Ezra, focused on Ezra's mission. The 58-year gap between chapters 6 and 7 (516-458 BC) is bridged by a single verse. explainer.summary: Ezra lists the family heads traveling with him, then discovers that no Levites have volunteered for the journey. He recruits Levites and temple servants. verses.explainer: They deliver the king's commissions to the satraps and governors of the province, who then support the people and the temple. Imperial authority serves divine purposes.

explainer.summary: Shortly after arriving in Jerusalem, Ezra is told that many Jews -- including priests, Levites, and leaders -- have intermarried with surrounding peoples. Ezra is devastated. He tears his garments, pulls out his hair, and sits in stunned silence until the evening sacrifice. Then he offers one of the most powerful prayers of confession in the Bible, acknowledging the community's sin and God's grace in giving them a remnant.
explainer.contextInBook: Ezra's intermarriage crisis reveals that 80 years of restoration have not solved the fundamental problem of covenant faithfulness. The community that rebuilt the temple has compromised its identity through marriages with peoples whose religions threaten to dilute Yahweh worship. Ezra's prayer is the theological response: honest confession, recognition of God's grace, and appeal for mercy.

wordStudies.significance: The people have 'mingled' with surrounding nations (v.2). The concern is not ethnic but religious: intermarriage with idolaters threatened to corrupt Yahweh worship from within.

Ezra repeatedly calls the community a 'remnant' (v.8, 13-15) -- survivors of exile who have been given a second chance. The term carries both humility (they are small) and hope (they have been preserved).
The intermarriage is described as ma'al (v.2, 4) -- treacherous unfaithfulness. The word implies betrayal of a covenant relationship, treating God as an enemy rather than a partner.

verses.text: Now when these things were done, the princes came near to me, saying, “The people of Israel, the priests, and the Levites, have not separated themselves from the peoples of the lands, following their abominations, even those of the Canaanites, the Hittites, the Perizzites, the Jebusites, the Ammonites, the Moabites, the Egyptians, and the Amorites.
verses.explainer: After Ezra's arrival, the community leaders inform him that the people of Israel -- including priests and Levites -- have not separated themselves from the surrounding peoples and their abominations.

annotations.word: the princes came near to me

crossReferences.connection: Moses explicitly warned against intermarriage with these peoples because 'they will turn your sons away from following me to serve other gods.'

verses.text: For they have taken of their daughters for themselves and for their sons, so that the holy offspring have mixed themselves with the peoples of the lands. Yes, the hand of the princes and rulers has been chief in this trespass.”
verses.explainer: The problem is specified: Jewish men have taken foreign wives, and the 'holy seed' has been mixed with the peoples of the lands. Worst of all, the leaders and officials led the way in this unfaithfulness.

annotations.word: the hand of the princes and rulers has been chief in this trespass

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The intermarriage concern was religious, not racial. Converts like Ruth were welcomed. The issue was marriage to those who would bring idolatrous worship practices into the community, threatening the fragile restoration of pure Yahweh worship.

thenVsNow.then: Then, intermarriage with idolaters threatened to dissolve the religious identity that exile should have purified. Today, faith communities still grapple with how to maintain distinctive identity while living graciously within a pluralistic society.
thenVsNow.now: Then, intermarriage with idolaters threatened to dissolve the religious identity that exile should have purified. Today, faith communities still grapple with how to maintain distinctive identity while living graciously within a pluralistic society.

verses.text: When I heard this thing, I tore my garment and my robe, and pulled the hair out of my head and of my beard, and sat down confounded.
verses.explainer: Ezra's reaction is extreme: he tears his garment and robe, pulls hair from his head and beard, and sits down in stunned grief. His physical actions express spiritual devastation.

annotations.word: tore my garment and my robe

verses.text: Then everyone who trembled at the words of the God of Israel were assembled to me, because of their trespass of the captivity; and I sat confounded until the evening offering.
verses.explainer: All who tremble at God's word gather around the stunned Ezra, and they sit together in silence until the evening sacrifice. The 'trembling' ones are the reform party.

annotations.word: everyone who trembled at the words of the God of Israel

verses.text: At the evening offering I arose up from my humiliation, even with my garment and my robe torn; and I fell on my knees, and spread out my hands to Yahweh my God;
verses.explainer: At the evening sacrifice, Ezra rises from his grief posture, kneels with hands spread toward heaven, and begins his prayer. The timing links personal prayer to communal worship.

annotations.word: fell on my knees, and spread out my hands to Yahweh

verses.text: and I said, “My God, I am ashamed and blush to lift up my face to you, my God; for our iniquities have increased over our head, and our guiltiness has grown up to the heavens.
verses.explainer: Ezra begins his prayer with personal shame: 'I am ashamed and embarrassed to lift my face to you, my God, because our iniquities have increased above our heads.'

annotations.word: our iniquities have increased over our head

verses.text: Since the days of our fathers we have been exceeding guilty to this day; and for our iniquities we, our kings, and our priests, have been delivered into the hand of the kings of the lands, to the sword, to captivity, to plunder, and to confusion of face, as it is this day.
verses.explainer: Ezra confesses that Israel's guilt extends from the time of the ancestors to the present. Their sins led to defeat by foreign kings, captivity, plunder, and shame.

verses.text: Now for a little moment grace has been shown from Yahweh our God, to leave us a remnant to escape, and to give us a nail in his holy place, that our God may lighten our eyes, and revived us a little in our bondage.
verses.explainer: Now, Ezra acknowledges, God has shown grace: a remnant has survived, God has given them a foothold in his holy place, and he has brightened their eyes and given them a little relief in their bondage.

annotations.word: a little moment grace has been shown from Yahweh

crossReferences.connection: Isaiah: 'Unless Yahweh of Hosts had left us a very small remnant, we would have been as Sodom.' Ezra echoes this remnant theology.

verses.text: For we are bondservants; yet our God has not forsaken us in our bondage, but has extended loving kindness to us in the sight of the kings of Persia, to revive us, to set up the house of our God, and to repair its ruins, and to give us a wall in Judah and in Jerusalem.
verses.explainer: Despite being servants of foreign kings, God has not forsaken them. He extended mercy through Persian kings to let them rebuild the temple and establish a 'wall' in Judah and Jerusalem.

verses.explainer: Having established God's grace, Ezra turns to the sin: 'What shall we say after this? For we have forsaken your commandments.'

verses.explainer: Ezra quotes (or paraphrases) prophetic warnings against intermarriage with the peoples of the land, whose practices have made the land unclean from end to end.

verses.text: Now therefore don’t give your daughters to their sons. Don’t take their daughters to your sons, nor seek their peace or their prosperity forever; that you may be strong, and eat the good of the land, and leave it for an inheritance to your children forever.’
verses.explainer: The prophetic instruction: do not give your daughters to their sons or take their daughters for your sons. Do not seek their peace or prosperity forever, so that you may be strong and eat the land's good things.

verses.text: “After all that has come on us for our evil deeds, and for our great guilt, since you, our God, have punished us less than our iniquities deserve, and have given us such a remnant,
verses.explainer: After everything that has happened -- exile and partial restoration -- the community is again violating the very commandments that caused the disaster. Ezra fears God's response will be even more severe.

verses.text: shall we again break your commandments, and join in affinity with the peoples that do these abominations? Wouldn’t you be angry with us until you had consumed us, so that there would be no remnant, nor any to escape?

verses.text: Yahweh, the God of Israel, you are righteous; for we are left a remnant that has escaped, as it is today. Behold, we are before you in our guiltiness; for no one can stand before you because of this.”

explainer.summary: Ezra's prayer and weeping attract a large assembly who also weep bitterly. Shecaniah proposes a radical solution: the community should make a covenant to put away their foreign wives and the children born to them. Ezra administers an oath, and a formal investigation begins. Over three months, the commission examines every case. The book ends with a list of the offending men from priestly, Levitical, and lay families.
themes: The painful cost of covenant renewal
The tension between mercy and covenant faithfulness
explainer.contextInBook: This deeply troubling chapter describes the dissolution of mixed marriages as the community's response to covenant unfaithfulness. Modern readers struggle with the human cost -- especially for the foreign wives and their children. The chapter raises difficult questions about how far covenant obedience should extend and what happens when communal identity conflicts with personal relationships.

wordStudies.translatedAs: To go out, send out, put forth
wordStudies.meaning: To go out, send out, put forth
wordStudies.significance: The foreign wives are to be 'sent away' or 'put out' (v.3, 19). The term is less harsh than divorce language but still describes the painful dissolution of marriages and separation of families.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Trembling, fearful, one who takes God's word serio
wordStudies.meaning: Trembling, fearful, one who takes God's word seriously
wordStudies.significance: The 'trembling' ones (charedim) who feared God's judgment formed the reform party (v.3). Their trembling before God's word drove the communal repentance.
The community makes a 'covenant' (berit) to put away the foreign wives (v.3). This formal agreement elevates the decision from individual choice to communal commitment.

verses.text: Now while Ezra prayed and made confession, weeping and casting himself down before God’s house, there was gathered together to him out of Israel a very great assembly of men and women and children; for the people wept very bitterly.
verses.explainer: While Ezra prays, confesses, and weeps prostrate before the temple, a very large assembly of men, women, and children gathers around him, also weeping bitterly. His grief becomes contagious.

verses.text: Shecaniah the son of Jehiel, one of the sons of Elam, answered Ezra, “We have trespassed against our God, and have married foreign women of the peoples of the land. Yet now there is hope for Israel concerning this thing.
verses.explainer: Shecaniah son of Jehiel speaks for the assembly, confessing their unfaithfulness but declaring hope: 'There is still hope for Israel concerning this thing.'

verses.text: Now therefore let us make a covenant with our God to put away all the wives, and those who as are born of them, according to the counsel of my lord, and of those who tremble at the commandment of our God. Let it be done according to the law.
verses.explainer: Shecaniah proposes the radical solution: make a covenant with God to put away all the foreign wives and their children, according to the counsel of those who tremble at God's commandment. Everything must be done according to the law.

according to the counsel of my lord

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: This proposal represents the most painful application of covenant priorities in the entire Bible. The separation of families was devastating, and modern readers rightly struggle with it. The text presents it as the community's decision driven by fear of God's judgment -- not as a model for all situations.

thenVsNow.then: Then, the community chose covenant faithfulness over family preservation, a devastatingly costly decision. Today, this passage raises difficult questions about the limits of religious obligation and how communities should handle conflicts between personal relationships and communal standards.
thenVsNow.now: Then, the community chose covenant faithfulness over family preservation, a devastatingly costly decision. verses.text: Then Ezra arose, and made the chiefs of the priests, the Levites, and all Israel, to swear that they would do according to this word. So they swore.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The abrupt ending -- with no note of comfort or resolution -- forces the reader to sit with the difficulty. The text does not celebrate the separations but presents them as the painful cost of covenant renewal. The silence about the fate of the women and children has troubled readers for millennia.

thenVsNow.then: Then, the community made the agonizing choice to dissolve marriages for the sake of covenant faithfulness. Today, this passage challenges us to consider what covenant obedience costs and to hold compassion and conviction together without easy answers.
